title: Learn how to insert, modify, and delete data in a DB with SQL
description: The examples in this post use the department, employee, project, and works_on tables we created in the previous installment. If you haven't read th...

children.value:
The examples in this post use the department, employee, project, and works_on tables we created in the previous installment.
If you haven't read the previous article, you should check it out.

The SQL statement that adds data is called an insert statement or insert query, and there are three ways to use it.
There are three ways to use it:
To use the way that uses all comma-separated values, write SQL like this

props.code: insert into table_name values (comma, separated, all, values);

children.value:
The order of the values you input follows the order of the attributes when you created the table.
Let's use the employee table we created in the previous post as an example.

props.code:
create table employee (
  id            int primary key,
  name          varchar(30) not null,
  birth_date    date,
  gender        char(1) check ( gender in ('M', 'F') ),
  position      varchar(10),
  salary        int,
  department_id int,
  foreign key (department_id) references department (id)
    on delete set null on update cascade,
  check ( salary >= 50000000 )
);

children.value: The attributes of the employee table are declared in the following order:

.
Let's insert the data in this order.

Consider the insert statements in lines 1 and 2.
We've specified the values in the order of the aforementioned attributes and returned a
result, so the data should have been successfully inserted.
One thing to note is that the value of

.
This is because the foreign key must refer to an existing value, but since there is no data in the department table yet, it is temporarily set to
The select statement in line 5 is the SQL that reads the value, which we'll cover in the next post.

Earlier, we saw a case where data is inserted normally.
This time, we'll create a case that violates a constraint, see the error message, and troubleshoot it.
An error occurred, and when I read the error message, I realized that the value of the primary key I was entering was a duplicate of a previously entered value, which violated the unique constraint.

Once again, an error occurred.
Reading the error message, we see that a check constraint was violated.
Since we used check constraints on both attributes,
, let's assume that we don't know which constraint was violated.
So let's check the name of the constraint using

props.code:
+----------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Table    | Create Table                                                                                                         |
+----------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| employee | CREATE TABLE `employee` (
  `id` int NOT NULL,
  `name` varchar(30) NOT NULL,
  `birth_date` date DEFAULT NULL,
  `gender` char(1) DEFAULT NULL,
  `position` varchar(10) DEFAULT NULL,
  `salary` int DEFAULT 50000000,
  `department_id` int DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `department_id` (`department_id`),
  CONSTRAINT `employee_ibfk_1` FOREIGN KEY (`department_id`) REFERENCES `department` (`id`) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE,
  CONSTRAINT `employee_chk_1` CHECK ((`gender` in (_latin1'M',_latin1'F'))),
  CONSTRAINT `employee_chk_2` CHECK ((`salary` >= 50000000))
) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_0900_ai_ci |
+----------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
1 row in set (0.00 sec)

children.value: As it turns out, the error was caused by the value of
So let's change the value of
to at least 50,000,000 and reinsert it.

props.code:
ERROR 1452 (23000): Cannot add or update a child row:
a foreign key constraint fails (`besfir_company`.`employee`, CONSTRAINT 
  `employee_ibfk_1` FOREIGN KEY (`department_id`) REFERENCES 
  `department` (`id`) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE)

children.value: This time, the query failed because it violated a foreign key constraint.
As mentioned earlier, the value of
must be a value that exists in the department table, but it doesn't yet, which violates the referential integrity constraint.
Since we don't have a value in the department table yet, we'll modify the value to
To specify a list of attributes, use the following SQL statement

props.code:
insert into table_name (comma, separated, attribute, list) 
values (comma, separated, values, in, attribute, order);

children.value:
Whereas the previous way requires you to specify values based on the table's definition, this way gives you flexibility in how you specify values.
Instead of having to enter all the values defined in the table, you can selectively insert only the properties you want, and you can reorder them.

props.code:
insert into employee (name, birth_date, gender, position, id)
values ('Smith', '2000-10-12', 'F', 'DEV_BACK', 3);

children.value: We have entered only some of the attributes defined in the table, and we are not following the order defined in the table.
We are entering values in the order of the selected attributes.
We haven't entered a value for
This is because we specified a default value of
Use the following bulk insert SQL statement

props.code:
insert into table_name (comma, separated, attribute, list)
    values
        (comma_1, separated_1, values_1, in_1, attribute_1, order_1),
        (comma_2, separated_2, values_2, in_2, attribute_2, order_2),
        ...;

children.value:
In a bulk insert, each record is separated by a comma, followed by a list of values enclosed in parentheses, listed row by row.
This format allows you to insert multiple records in a single INSERT query.
Let's use it to insert additional employee data.

props.code:
Query OK, 11 rows affected (0.01 sec)
Records: 11  Duplicates: 0  Warnings: 0

children.value: Now that you know how to use it, let's populate the rest of the tables we created in the previous article.
When updating data, write SQL like this
You can specify one or more table names.
The attributes and values you want to modify act as a pair, and you can also specify more than one.
If you specify multiple tables, you may get an
unless you explicitly specify the properties in the form
The where clause specifies the condition of the records to be targeted and can be omitted or multiple.
If you omit it, all records will be updated.
Now that we have stored the values in all tables, we can assign a value to the

attribute of the employee table.
Let's use an update query to modify the value of the
attribute of the first record with an id value of

props.code:
Query OK, 1 row affected (0.01 sec)
Rows matched: 1  Changed: 1  Warnings: 0

children.value:
The result was returned with a message that 1 row was affected.
Let's verify that this is indeed the case.

To ensure that we only output the records we modified, we specified an ID value in the WHERE clause, and when we checked, the UPDATE query was applied correctly.
Now let's modify the rest of the records.

Now let's create a hypothetical scenario and practice.
The head of Company wants to increase the salary of all employees in the development department to double their current salary.
Let's reflect this with an update query.

props.code:
Query OK, 5 rows affected (0.01 sec)
Rows matched: 5  Changed: 5  Warnings: 0

children.value:
The resulting message shows that five records have been modified.
This time, we're going to double the salary of all employees working on a project with a project ID of 2003.

props.code:
update employee, works_on
set salary = salary * 2
where id = employee_id
  and project_id = 2003;

children.value:
Since the employee and works_on tables do not have overlapping attribute names, the above expression is fine.
However, when using multiple tables, overlapping attribute names can lead to ambiguous errors, and it is often difficult to tell which table's attribute belongs to which table by looking at the table specification.
Therefore, when using multiple tables, it is better to specify the table names and their attributes as shown below.

props.code:
update employee, works_on
set employee.salary = employee.salary * 2
where employee.id = works_on.employee_id
  and works_on.project_id = 2003;

children.value: Line 1: To update the salary, we need to select the employee table, but we don't know the number of projects each employee is working on.
So we do an implicit join between the employee table, which contains the salary information, and the works_on table, which contains the employee and project numbers.
The resulting table is a Cartesian product, which is quite far from reality. If you're curious, you can try
Line 3: Select only the information where
from the table represented by the Cartesian product to get the information that matches reality.
Line 4 : Select only the records where
This time, we're going to double the salaries of all employees.
Since we're not restricting the condition with a WHERE clause, it will be reflected on all records in the table.
When deleting data, write SQL like this
You should also be careful with delete queries, as they can delete all records if you don't specify a where clause.

Let's practice this by creating a hypothetical scenario.
Nicole is leaving the company and you need to delete her information from all tables.
Nicole's employee ID is 8 and she was part of project 2002.
The tables we need to delete information from are employee and works_on.
We first deleted Nicole's information from the employee table.
But does the works_on table also need to be deleted?
The lookup shows that Nicole's information already deleted in the works_on table.
The reason is the reference option of the foreign key.

props.code:
create table works_on (
  employee_id int,
  project_id  int,
  primary key (employee_id, project_id),
  foreign key (employee_id) references employee (id)
    on delete cascade on update cascade,
  foreign key (project_id) references project (id)
    on delete cascade on update cascade
);

children.value: This is because when we created the table, we specified the cascade option so that when material is deleted from the referenced table, it is also deleted from the works_on table.

Jane is taking a leave of absence and is dropping out of her current project.
Jane's ID is 2, and she was a participant in projects 2001, 2002, and 2003.
Instead of having to specify each
and do it all at once.

Messi is currently working on three projects and has decided to drop out of projects 2002 and 2003 to focus on project 2001.
Messi's ID is 5.

props.code:
delete from works_on where employee_id = 5 and project_id = 2002
delete from works_on where employee_id = 5 and project_id = 2003

children.value: We could run a delete query for every
However, this leaves a lot of room for error as the number of records we need to delete grows.

props.code:
delete from works_on where employee_id = 5 and project_id <> 2001
-- or
delete from works_on where employee_id = 5 and project_id != 2001

children.value: allows you to delete only records with project IDs other than 2001 at once.
The company has a big problem and closes all projects.
If you don't use a where clause, you can delete the entire data in the table.

title: Learn the basic concepts of SQL and the basic SQL syntax that defines the structure of a DB
description: SQL Structured Query Language (SQL) is the standard language for relational database management systems (RDBMS) used in the industry. It can also b...

children.value:
Structured Query Language (SQL) is the standard language for relational database management systems (RDBMS) used in the industry.
It can also be referred to as a comprehensive DBMS language that combines DDL, DML, and VDL.
The key terms are listed below, but they are more often used interchangeably, so keep them in mind.
What is a relation in SQL?

In a relational data model, a relation does not allow duplicate tuples.
However, in SQL, a relation is a multiset (=bag) of tuples, which means it

.
Therefore, we can understand that a table in SQL can have duplicate records.

SQL is the standard language for RDBMSs, but since there is no mandatory implementation, the specifications provided by each RDBMS are slightly different.
For this reason, the standard SQL is called

or ISO SQL, and the implementation of each RDBMS is called vendor SQL.
A vendor is a supplier of a product or service, in this case, a specific RDBMS manufacturer.

Let's see how to define a DB by creating an RDB for an IT company.
In this article, we'll create a relational database where we can define information about departments, employees, and projects.
For the tutorial, we'll use the InnoDB engine, the most popular of MySQL's engines.
First, let's see what databases currently exist by running the

command to see what databases currently exist.
Since we haven't created any databases yet, we should be able to see the default created database.

props.code:
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| information_schema |
| mysql              |
| performance_schema |
| sys                |
+--------------------+
4 rows in set (0.01 sec)

children.value:
command to create a database of IT companies, which is our goal.
I will name the database besfir_company.

props.code:
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| besfir_company     |
| information_schema |
| mysql              |
| performance_schema |
| sys                |
+--------------------+
5 rows in set (0.00 sec)

children.value: to see which database is currently selected.

, which means there is no database specified to use yet.
Now let's specify the database we created earlier as the database to use.

Notice that besfir_company is specified as the database to use.
Now it's time to define the tables, but before we do that, let's learn how to delete the database.
We'll create a separate database to test the deletion and then delete it.

props.code:
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| besfir_company     |
| delete_test        |
| information_schema |
| mysql              |
| performance_schema |
| sys                |
+--------------------+
6 rows in set (0.00 sec)

mysql> show databases;
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| besfir_company     |
| information_schema |
| mysql              |
| performance_schema |
| sys                |
+--------------------+
5 rows in set (0.01 sec)

children.value:
In MySQL, database and schema are synonymous.
Therefore, the schema keyword can be used in place of the database keyword, such as

.
On the other hand, in other RDBMSs, they have different meanings.
For example, in PostgreSQL, schema is used for the namespace of the database.

props.code:
-- in MySQL
create database besfir_company = create schema besfir_company

children.value:
This also leads to differences in where tables are defined.
In MySQL, tables are defined in the database, whereas in PostgreSQL, tables are defined in the schema within the database.
of the previous relation schema article that if an attribute has an underscore, it is a primary key. With that in mind, let's check out the relation schema.
id : Primary key for unique identification.
name : The name of the department.
leader_id : Unique identification value of the department leader. Refer to the id value of EMPLOYEE.
, name, birth_date, gender, position, salary, department_id)
id: Primary key for unique identification.
department_id: Department unique identification value. Refer to id value of DEPARTMENT.
id: Primary key for unique identification.
leader_id : Unique identification value of project manager. Refer to id value of EMPLOYEE.
employee_id : Unique identification value of an employee who participated in the project. Refer to the id value of EMPLOYEE.
project_id : Unique ID identification value of the project. Refer to the id value of PROJECT.
This time, we'll create the table we designed earlier using SQL.
column is an integer (int) data type that serves as the primary key and uniquely identifies each row.
column is a string (varchar) data type of up to 20 characters, does not accept NULL, and has a unique value.
column is an integer (int) data type.

props.code:
create table employee (
  id            int primary key,
  name          varchar(30) not null,
  birth_date    date,
  gender        char(1) check ( gender in ('M', 'F') ),
  position      varchar(10),
  salary        int,
  department_id int,
  foreign key (department_id) references department (id)
    on delete set null on update cascade,
  check ( salary >= 50000000 )
);

children.value: column accepts a letter as a value, and only accepts 'M' or 'F' as a value through attribute-level validation.
is for a foreign key that references another table. The department_id column references the id column of the department table.
When a specific record is deleted from the department table, the value of the department_id column in the employee table that references it is modified to NULL.
If the id value in the department table changes, the department_id value in the employee table that references it is also modified.
column is validated at the table level to allow values greater than 50000000.

props.code:
create table project (
  id         int primary key,
  name       varchar(20) not null unique,
  leader_id  int,
  start_date date,
  end_date   date,
  foreign key (leader_id) references employee (id)
    on delete set null on update cascade,
  check ( start_date < project.end_date )
);

create table works_on (
  employee_id int,
  project_id  int,
  primary key (employee_id, project_id),
  foreign key (employee_id) references employee (id)
    on delete cascade on update cascade,
  foreign key (project_id) references project (id)
    on delete cascade on update cascade
);

children.value: The project and works_on tables will not be described because they overlap with the previous ones.

We've defined all the tables we need, but we've missed something.
We didn't declare a foreign key on the leader_id of the department table.
This is because the employee table didn't exist when we created the department table, so we couldn't declare a foreign key on it.
This time, let's apply the foreign key using the

props.code:
alter table department add foreign key (leader_id)
  references employee(id)
  on update cascade 
  on delete set null;

children.value:
We are now done defining your tables.
In the next post, you'll learn how to manipulate data through these tables.
- Used for storing fixed-point numbers accurately.
MySQL implements all integer types according to the SQL standard.
PostgreSQL does not have TINYINT and MEDIUMINT types, only
and specifies the number of whole digits, and 2 is called
and specifies the number of decimal places.
In the SQL standard, DECIMAL provides a flexible type, so even if a six-digit number is entered in

, it will store a value.
NUMERIC, on the other hand, is strictly typed and will not store a value.
MySQL doesn't distinguish between DECIMAL and NUMERIC types and behaves as a NUMERIC, so it doesn't save if the digits don't match.
- Specifies the maximum number of 'characters' to store in the string.
- If the length of the stored string is smaller than the maximum length, the remaining space is filled with spaces and stored.
- Specifies the maximum number of 'characters' to store in the string.
- Stores only the length of the string being stored.

VARCHAR is recommended for PostgreSQL because unlike the performance benefits of CHAR in other RDBMSs, it can be slower in PostgreSQL at the cost of additional storage and some additional computation.
MySQL recommends using CHAR for data with fixed string sizes, such as phone numbers, and VARCHAR for attributes with variable string sizes.
In PostgreSQL, there is only one of the four types, the TEXT type.

In MySQL, TINYTEXT and TEXT can store strings of less than or equal to the length of a CHAR or VARCHAR
Therefore, you should use MEDIUMTEXT or LONGTEXT to store large strings.
, which represents up to three digits of the hour, this can be used to indicate the passage of time.
Date and Time data type additional explanation.

For TIMESTAMPs in MySQL, you can either adhere to the OS's timezone or specify a timezone within MySQL.
Upon storage, values are recorded in standard time.
When retrieving values, they are converted to reflect the designated time zone and retrieved accordingly.
- MySQL doesn't have a separate boolean type.
Usually used to store encryption keys

A primary key is used to identify a record in a table and consists of one or more attributes.
The primary key must be unique and cannot have NULL as a value.
Here's how to declare a primary key.
When composed of a single attribute
When composed of one or more attributes
When specifying a single attribute as the primary key, either method is acceptable.
When specifying more than one attribute as the primary key, it must be specified in the form primary

Attributes with unique constraints cannot have duplicate values.
However, the treatment of NULL values can vary depending on the RDBMS.
While MySQL and PostgreSQL allow duplicates of NULLs for columns with unique constraints, Microsoft SQL Server (MsSQL) does not allow duplicates of NULLs and allows only one NULL to exist per column.
Here's how to declare UNIQUE constraint.
When composed of a single attribute
When composed of one or more attributes
If a attribute is specified as NOT NULL, the attribute cannot have a NULL value.
Here's how to declare NOT NULL constraint.

Usually, the "NOT NULL" constraint is used in conjunction with "UNIQUE."
This is done to prevent duplicate NULL values and to ensure that NULL values do not exist.
Use this when you want to limit the value of a attribute.
When composed of a single attribute
When composed of one or more attributes
As shown above, check constraints are used to validate and restrict the data entered, preventing input that doesn't meet business rules.
Used when a attribute refers to a primary or unique key of another table.
The department_id column in the EMPLOYEE table is referencing the id column in the DEPARTMENT table.
In this case, the department_id column of the EMPLOYEE table can only refer to values that exist in the id column of the DEPARTMENT table.
Therefore, 14 in the department_id column is a non-existent DEPARTMENT id value, which
Here's how to declare a foreign key.
Reflects deletion/update of referenced value as is
Reflects deletion/update of referenced value as NULL
Changes referenced value to default on deletion/update

: When a record is deleted from DEPARTMENT, the referencing EMPLOYEE records are also deleted.
If the id value of a DEPARTMENT record is changed to 1, the department_id value of the referencing EMPLOYEE records is also changed to 1.
: When a DEPARTMENT record is deleted or its id value is changed, the department_id value of the referencing EMPLOYEE records is set to NULL.
: If the record is referenced by other tables, deletion of the DEPARTMENT record or modification of specific columns is prohibited.
: In MySQL, it is the same as RESTRICT. In standard SQL, within a transaction, deletion/modification is allowed, but it's prohibited after the transaction ends.
: When the referenced value is deleted/changed, it is changed to the default value. This is not supported in MySQL.
In MySQL, only CASCADE, SET NULL, and RESTRICT are supported. In PostgreSQL, all five options are supported.

By naming them, you can easily identify which constraints have been violated.
Also, if you want to delete a constraint, you can do so by its name.
For MySQL, the validation message with and without constraints is shown below.
If you don't specify a constraint name, it becomes difficult to identify which constraint has been violated. In other words, it leads to a less intuitive design.
In the case of MySQL, if you want to know what the 'test_chk_1' constraint is, you can find out by entering show create table test;.

Used to define the default value of a attribute.
When saving a new record, if there is no input value for the attribute, it will be saved with the default value.
Since the price was not specified, the "price" attribute of the record will be inserted with the default value of 10,000.

It is used to change the schema of a table in an RDBMS.
There are many different types of ALTER TABLE statements, and we'll look at some examples below.
If you're changing the schema of a table that's already in service, it's important to make sure you've thought through the impact of your changes before making them.
It is used to delete a table and is described below.
Important points when defining the DB structure
It is important to define the DB schema appropriately by considering the specifications of the service you want to create, as well as data consistency, convenience, and scalability.

title: Let's learn about the View Transition API
description: Chrome 111 introduced an interesting feature called the "View Transition API". In this article, we'll take a look at the View Transition API. What...

children.value:
Since the version Chrome 111, we was added an interesting feature called the "View Transition API".
In this article, we'll take a look at the View Transition API.
What is the View Transition API?

The View Transition API provides the ability to easily create animated transitions between different DOM states while updating DOM content at once.
Let's take a look at some examples of where it can be used.

If the order of elements changes due to user interaction, it may be difficult to identify which elements have changed.
In this case, a reordering animation can help the user recognize that the order has changed.
In the example above, the View Transition API is applied to the draggable elements to make the reordering easier to recognize.

props.title: Page transition effects for server-side rendering

children.value:
SSRs that utilize backend frameworks are MPAs, which means that it takes a lot more work to implement page transition animation effects compared to SPAs.
However, by utilizing the View Transition API, it is relatively easy to implement page transition effects.
In the demo video above, we applied the View Transition API to Express + Handlebars to implement the page transition effect, and the video plays uninterrupted even when the page is switched.
💡 As this is still an experimental feature, it only works with Chrome, Edge, and Opera, which are Chromium Engine-based browsers.

For practice, we've created a simple example that toggles the shape of a shape when a button is clicked.
We'll build on this example and add some code to demonstrate how to use it.

The basic usage is really simple.
Just update the DOM inside
and the transition will be applied immediately.

directly on click to updating via the View Transition API.
If you click the button, you can see that the default transition effect, cross-fade, is applied.
The View Transition API can be summarized in the following steps.
is called, the API takes a screenshot of the current page. 2.
is called to change the DOM.
Capture the new state of the changed page as a live representation.
Construct a pseudo-element tree with the following structure.
is the top-level pseudo-element that contains all View Transition pseudo-element overlays.
is a screenshot of the old page we worked on in
is a live representation captured in
Both of these render as content similar to
, so they can be styled with attributes like
The default effect, a cross-fade, is applied to

This time, let's replace the default transition with a different effect.
We want the pre-update elements to disappear as they fade out to the left, and the post-update elements to appear as they fade in from the right.

I hadn't noticed it before, but when I checked the results, I realized that the transition effect was also applied to the header.
It's a little awkward to have transitions applied to the header when you're navigating through a web page.
The previous example pointed out some awkwardness, so let's create two requirements and modify them accordingly.
I don't want to apply a transition to the header before and after a DOM update because it doesn't change.
I want the button to move naturally as the size of the shape changes.
value, it will smoothly transition to the same element before and after the DOM update.
Headers will remain as they are because there is no pre- or post-DOM update call to smoothly transition them.
The button will be repositioned up or down after the DOM update, depending on the size of the shape, so it will be moved to provide a smooth transition.

corresponding to that value.
This makes it possible to implement different effects for different elements because they act as separate overlays.

value must be unique, and if there are duplicate values within a single page, the transition effect will be ignored.
You can also give shapes a unique

This is a quick overview of the View Transition API.
In the next post, we'll apply the View Transition API to an MPA rendered with Express + Handlebars to implement a page transition effect.
Smooth and simple transitions with the View Transitions API

links.text: What is the View Transition API?

title: Let's learn about the relational database
description: In this article, we'll learn about the concept of a relational database, including the terms relation, primary key, foreign key, and constraints. T...

children.value: In this article, we'll learn about the concept of a relational database, including the terms relation, primary key, foreign key, and constraints.
The concept of relational databases comes from mathematics, so to make it easier to understand, we'll start by looking at the concept of relational databases in math.
A set is a collection of different elements, characterized by the fact that the order of the elements in a set does not matter.
The set above satisfies the definition of a set because there are no duplicates.
It doesn't matter if we write it as
Because of the order-insensitive nature, the meaning doesn't change because of the reordering.

props.alt: Cartesian product A × B = {(a, b) | a ∈ A and b ∈ B }

children.value: The set of all sets that can be represented by selecting one element from each of the sets A and B in the figure above is called a cartesian product and can be symbolized as

.
The reason for the sudden mention of the Cartesian product is that it is related to the relation in math.
When a relationship is formed by selecting one element from each of

, it is called a binary relation.
Let's look at the figure above to understand binary relations.
Let's represent a binary relation with the symbol
This shows that a binary relation R is a subset of the Cartesian product of sets A and B.

, it is called an n-ary relation, which is a subset of the Cartesian product over n sets.
Let's generalize and write an n-ary relation.
`R' consists of three ordered pair sets.
{2, r, ..., β}`, are each called a tuple.
In this case, the set is called an n-tuple because it is a set of n elements.
A tuple is a concept used to represent an association between two or more sets.
is an n-tuple of three sets of order pairs.
Based on the above, a relation in mathematics can be expressed as follows

Now that we've looked at relationships in math, let's apply them to the relational data model.
Sets, set names, tuples, and relations in math correspond to domains, domain names, tuples, and relations in the relational data model, respectively.
Here, a domain is a set of attribute values.
To make it easier to understand, let's create a student relation example.
Let's define the student domains as follows
students_ids : set of student numbers, 7-digit integer.
human_names: set of human names, string.
major_names: a set of names of majors studied at the university.
phone_numbers : Set of cell phone numbers.

Earlier, we said that a domain is a set of attribute values.
However, if you look at the domain definitions above, you'll notice that they specify the type of the values.
For example,

is a string or natural number depending on the definition.
They also inherit the characteristics of the set in common, so no duplicate data can be used, which can also be seen as a type that does not allow duplication.
In this way, domains can also be seen as defining the types of values that each attribute can have.

We'll use the domain we defined earlier to represent the student relation.
Since we don't yet know how to represent the relation other than the mathematical representation we practiced earlier, we'll make it as mathematical as possible.

props.alt: Mathematically representing a relational data model

children.value:
As I was creating it, something changed.
In addition to the student's phone number, I realized that I needed an emergency contact number.
So we used the phone number domain twice to represent this.
However, domains alone don't tell you which domain is the student's phone number or the emergency contact number.

We mentioned earlier that a domain can also be seen as defining the type of attribute value.
In this case, the type of value is specified through the domain name.
Therefore, the domain name only specifies the type of the domain, not its purpose.
This is where attributes come in.
The attribute is used to express the name of the role that the domain plays in the relation.
By using an attribute, we can distinguish between a student's phone number and an emergency contact in the image above, which use the same phone number domain.

As we saw in the previous section on relation in math, the association between each set can be represented as a tuple, which in the figure is a 6-tuple.
As the degree of the tuple increases and the number of order pairs increases, the mathematical representation of the tuple becomes very complex.
Therefore, in the relational data model, relation is represented by a table because it is the easiest way to represent tuples.

props.alt: Representing relational data models as tables

children.value:
Sometimes a relation is an abstract concept that refers to a table, as above, but sometimes it refers to a relation state, as below.
In this case, a relation is a set of tuples at a point in time, i.e., actual data at a point in time.
However, the difference in semantics is minimal, and you shouldn't have any trouble understanding the context either way.

Domain: An atomic set of values that is no longer divisible, defining constraints on data types and attributes.
It specifies the valid values that can be assigned to attributes.
domain name: The name assigned to a domain, used to define the range of values in the domain and to constrain the values of its attributes.

attribute: The name of the role the domain plays in the relationship.
Each attribute represents a specific column in the table and can only have values defined for that domain.

tuple: A unit of data that represents each row in a table, and is an ordered pair of values containing the value of each attribute.
Also known as a record, some values can be NULL.
I will refer to them as records when referring to rows in the relational data model, and tuples when bringing them into a programming language.
relation name: serves as the name of the table, indicating what subject or object the table contains information about.
relation: an abstract concept that combines a set of tuples (=relationship state) and attributes.

Represents the structure of a relation, represented as a list of relation names and attributes.
It also includes constraints associated with the attributes.

props.code: STUDENT(id, name, grade, major, phone_num, emer_phone_num)

children.value: STUDENT(id, name, grade, major, phone_num, emer_phone_num)
The degree of a relation is the number of attributes in the relation schema.

props.code: STUDENT(id, name, grade, major, phone_num, emer_phone_num) -> degree 6

children.value: STUDENT(id, name, grade, major, phone_num, emer_phone_num) -> degree 6

It is a structured database based on a relational data model.
This relational database is composed of multiple relations.

A relational database schema is a combination of a set of relational schemas and a set of integrity constraints.
It is an important concept that defines the structure and integrity of a database.
A relation cannot have duplicate records.
Since a relation is a set of records, it inherits the properties of a set and cannot have duplicate records.
Set a subset of attributes as keys to identify records in the relation.
The example below uses the id attribute.
The order of the records in a relation doesn't matter.

Reordering the records does not change the meaning of the records.
Therefore, we don't define how the order of the records should be displayed.
An attribute's name cannot be duplicated in a single relation.
The order of the attributes in a record does not matter.
The order doesn't change the meaning.
The attribute must be an atomic value that is no longer divisible.

Composite properties, where a single property is composed of multiple subproperties, are not allowed.
Also, multivalued attributes, where a single attribute can have multiple values and these values can exist independently, are not allowed.
The address attribute, Cheongdam-dong, Gangnam-gu, Seoul, Korea, is a composite attribute because it can have city, district, state, and country subattributes.
Major attributes composite and architecture can be separated because they are double major.
How to separate composite and multivalued properties will be covered in another post.
NULL has a very ambiguous meaning.
The value exists, but we don't know what it is yet.
For the TOEIC score attribute, NULL could mean the following
You haven't taken the test yet.
The transfer attribute also has an ambiguous meaning for NULL, which could be
Not applicable because you haven't transferred.
As you can see, the semantics of NULL are very ambiguous and should be avoided.
A set of attributes that uniquely identify the records in a relation.
For PLAYER(id, name, team_id, back_number, birth_date).
The super key is {id, name, team_id, back_number, birth_date}, {id, name}, {name, team_id, back_number}, etc.

A super key that cannot uniquely identify a record by removing any of its attributes.
It is also called a key or minimal super key.
For PLAYER(id, name, team_id, back_number, birth_date).
{id}: The id value can identify the record because it is unique on its own, and removing that attribute would make it indistinguishable, so it is a candidate key.

{team_id, back_number} : Since there are multiple players on a team, the team_id attribute is not enough to distinguish them.
The back_number attribute is also indistinguishable because there may be players with the same number on different teams.
Therefore, removing either of them makes the record unidentifiable, making it a candidate key.
The candidate key selected to uniquely identify a record in the relation.
For PLAYER(id, name, team_id, back_number, birth_date).
The primary key is {id} or {team_id, back_number}.
It is usually more convenient to set {id} as the primary key because it is more convenient to use a smaller number of attributes.
The attribute selected as the primary key is underlined.

A candidate key that is not the primary key.
Sometimes called an alternate key.
Since the id attribute is underscored, {id} is the primary key, so {team_id, back_number} is the unique key.
A set of attributes that refer to the PK of another relationship, abbreviated as FK.
, name, team_id, back_number, birth_date) and TEAM(
The foreign key is {team_id} for PLAYER.
A constraint that a relation in a relational database must always honor.
This is a constraint imposed by the relational data model itself.
A relation cannot have duplicate records.
You can't have properties with the same name within a relation.

Constraints that can be specified directly in the schema, usually via DDL.
Also called explicit constraints.

The value of an attribute must be a value that belongs to the domain of that attribute.
In the example below, grade 56 violates the constraint because it is outside the scope of the domain.

Different records cannot have keys with the same value.
The example below violates the constraint because it uses the same primary key.
If an attribute is defined as not null, it cannot have a value of NULL.
A primary key cannot have a value of NULL.
💡 An entity is a representation of an object or concept that you want to store in a database, and is synonymous with table and relation.
The FK and PK must have the same domain, and the FK cannot have a value that is not in the PK.

title: Let's learn about the basic concepts of database part 2
description: Database schema A database schema, or schema for short, defines and represents the structure of a database based on a data model. The schema is det...

children.value:
A database schema, or schema for short, defines and represents the structure of a database based on a data model.
The schema is determined early in the design of a database and is not often changed once it is established.

A schematized schema is called a schema diagram and is a powerful tool that helps you visually represent the relationships between tables, as shown in the figure below.
Schema diagrams play an important role in the design and implementation of databases and help in the development and maintenance of database systems.

The actual data in a database can change quite often.
The data in a database at any given time is called the database state or database snapshot.
Another way to say it is a set of the current instances in the database.
Database state includes the contents and attributes of all tables in the database, relationships between tables, transaction information, log information, and more.
💡 A database instance is a state in which a DBMS is loaded into memory and running at any given time.

It is one of the architectures for building database systems and has the purpose of separating the physical database from the user application.
The model has three levels, each of which has a defined schema.
This isolation is used to increase reliability by ensuring that changes at any level do not affect the higher levels.

However, most modern DBMSs do not completely or explicitly divide the three phases.
This is because it is not practical to ensure that changes at each level do not affect the higher levels.
Even though we've divided the schema into three levels, the internal level is where the actual data resides.
💡 User applications refer to software used by general users or individuals or companies that handle tasks, and act as clients of database systems.
External schemas at external level (or User views at view level)

It expresses only the data that specific users need, and hides data that they don't need to know.
This schema is expressed through a logical data model.
It is also called external views, user views, and sub schema, among other names.
The above description is a bit confusing, but in a nutshell, it means
a tabular representation of the structure of a database that a user or application sees in response to a query in the database

It describes the structure of the entire database, focusing on entities, data types, associations, user operations, and constraints.
However, it hides information about the physical storage structure.
An example of this schema is the ER diagram, which, like the external schema, is represented through a logical data model.
A conceptual schema is a database structure designed by a DBA or developer in the form of tables.

💡 A user operation is an action that a user or application performs to interact with a database.
There can be many different types of user operations, but they mainly include the following tasks

💡 An entity is a concept used to represent a conceptual object in database design, while a table is a structure that actually physically stores data.
Entities and tables may not always correspond. (For example, inheritance structures)

Represent how data is physically stored through a physical data model.
It describes tangible things like data storage, data structures, and access paths.
The main goal of this schema is to improve the performance and efficiency of the database by optimizing physical storage and processing.

Let's look at the difference between a logical and physical data model with an example code.
The following example assumes a logical data model for storing student grades.
Now, if we were to implement this logical data model as a physical data model, we might add a physical table structure, index structure, physical placement of data files, etc.

props.code:
Physical Table Structure:
- Students (Table):
  +--------------+-------------+---------------+
  | Student_ID   | Student_Name| Major         |
  +--------------+-------------+---------------+
  | 101 (Offset) | John Doe    | CS (Offset)   |
  | 102 (Offset) | Jane Smith  | History (Offset)|
  | 103 (Offset) | Bob Johnson | Physics (Offset)|
  | ...          | ...         | ...           |
  +--------------+-------------+---------------+

Index Structure:
- Index on Student_ID:
  +--------------+-------------+
  | Student_ID   | Offset      |
  +--------------+-------------+
  | 101          | Offset_1    |
  | 102          | Offset_2    |
  | 103          | Offset_3    |
  | ...          | ...         |
  +--------------+-------------+

Physical Storage on Disk:
- Students Data File:
  1. Offset_1: [101][John Doe][CS]
  2. Offset_2: [102][Jane Smith][History]
  3. Offset_3: [103][Bob Johnson][Physics]
  ...

children.value:
It is a language used to define conceptual schemas, and in some cases even internal schemas.
Examples include creating, modifying, and deleting tables, creating indexes, and creating databases.

A language used to define internal schemas.
Nowadays, especially in relational DBMSs, SDL is rarely used and is replaced by settings such as parameters.

A language used to define an external schema.
In most DBMSs, DDL also fulfills the role of VDL.

A language for utilizing data in a database.
It provides the ability to add, delete, modify, and search data.

Today's DBMSs exist as a unified language rather than DML, VDL, and DDL.
A prime example of this is the relational data language - SQL.
Distinguish between external schema, conceptual schema, and internal schema!

children.text: External schemas at external level (or User views at view level)

title: Let's learn about the basic concepts of database part 1
description: Database design is so difficult that when I tried to organize it, I realized that I don't know anything about database basics. So, before I start w...

children.value:
Database design is so difficult that when I tried to organize it, I realized that I don't know anything about database basics.
So, before I start writing the Database Design series, I'm going to summarize the Getting Started with Databases series.
In this article, I'll summarize the information you need to get started with databases.

Consider a social networking service as an example.
Users post photos, write posts, comment on other users' content, and more.
As such, data that has the same origin and purpose within the same service is called related data.

Imagine that your books are strewn across the floor.
It's hard to find them quickly, and it's hard to tell if you have duplicates of the same book.
But if you organize them and put them on a bookshelf, you can quickly find the book you want and easily see if you have unnecessary duplicates.
A set that organizes data in this way to increase efficiency and data accessibility is called an organized set.

It means storing data efficiently and keeping it on a computer where it can be quickly retrieved.
This allows for fast computation and retrieval while taking up little space.
Software that provides users with the ability to

, create, and manage databases, most commonly PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, and MsSQL.
In the process of defining a database, the structure of the data is determined, and this process generates additional data, which is called metadata.
The term is widely used in a variety of fields, and its original meaning is "

," meaning that it refers to data that describes or characterizes data.
For example, when you take a photo, you may see additional information that describes the photo, such as the date it was taken, the file format, the resolution, etc.

In the database world, it refers to the data that defines or describes a database, which is sometimes referred to as a catalog.
The catalog can refer to the metadata itself, but it can also refer to the place where the metadata is stored and managed by the DBMS.
Examples of metadata include data types, structures, constraints, security, storage, indexes, and user groups.

it refers to the Database + DBMS + associated applications.
However, if you read several documents, you may shorten the database system and call it just database.
Therefore, it is necessary to understand the meaning by carefully grasping the context of whether the database of the document means pure data or database system.
Users / Programmers: The application is used by users or programmers.

Application Programs / Queries: The application sends queries to the DBMS to perform some function requested by the user.
In this context, a query is a request to get the desired data from the database, or to modify or delete it in a desired form.
Software to Process Queries / Programs: Analyze what the query means.
Software to Access Stored Data: Once it understands what is being requested, it processes the request.
Stored Database Definition: DBMS needs to know the metadata before we can process the data, so we find and verify the metadata first.
Stored Database: Process the desired data based on the metadata and return the results to the application.

A set of concepts that can be used to describe the structure of a database.
Based on those concepts, it provides a way to

the structure of a database and represent it in a desired form.
The data model also includes the basic operations for reading and writing to the database.
The database structure mentioned refers to concepts that include data types, relationships between data, constraints, etc.
There are several types of data models, each with a slightly different level of abstraction and a different way of structuring the database.

It is a model consisting of concepts that can be easily understood by not only developers but also ordinary users, and has the highest level of abstraction.
This data model is used to describe business requirements at an abstract level.
A representative data model is the
, and its visual representation is the Entity-Relationship Diagram, or ER Diagram for short.
The figure below shows an ER diagram of the relationship between a student and a book, assuming you are building a university library system.

It provides concepts that allow you to structure your database in a way that is not too difficult to understand.
Structuring the database in detail means structuring the logical data model so that it is not significantly different from the structure of the data model stored on the computer.
However, this model does not even represent how data is stored on a physical storage device, but rather is a model that can structure the database at a level that is not dependent on a specific DBMS or storage.

The main data model is the relational data model, and other data models include the object data model and the object-relational data model.
Among the major DBMSs mentioned above, Oracle, MySQL, and MsSQL are based on the relational data model.
And PostgreSQL is based on an object-relational data model but includes a relational data model, so it can be said that the major DBMSs are based on a relational data model.
series, we'll focus on this relational data model.

As can be seen in the figure above, relationship simply means a table.
In a table, row means one data and is called a tuple or record.
In addition, a column is called an attribute and refers to the characteristics of a tuple or record.

It provides a way to describe how data is stored in files on a computer.
In other words, it is a data model that expresses elements such as data format, data orderings, and access paths to represent the database structure as closely as possible to the way data is actually stored on the storage device.
Among them, access paths are structures to speed up data retrieval, such as indexes.
